Eligibility 3.1 This procedure is available to students registered on a programme of study at the University of Essex, wishing to raise a concern or complaint regarding an issue by which they have been materially affected. The term ‘student’ includes those registered to study with the University of Essex or registered for its awards and those who have recently left the University. Those students who have recently left a programme of study may only raise issues of concern or complaint within three months after the conferral of an award or withdrawal from their programme. The Academic Registrar (or nominee) has discretion to extend this three month period where they consider that there are compelling reasons to do so. 3.2 Some issues may be more appropriately considered under processes other than this procedure. As such, this procedure will not normally cover: ◼ appeals relating to examinations or assessments or to academic progress or against

which should be submitted to the Academic Registrar via the Student Progress Team. The University of Essex will accept complaints about free speech within the institution from both current and former students, as well as from staff members (including applicants for academic posts) and visiting speakers (including those who have been invited to be visiting speakers). following their disclosure; any victimisation may result in disciplinary action for the individual involved. 3.5 It should be emphasised that this policy is intended to assist individuals who believe they have discovered malpractice or serious wrongdoing provided that they make the disclosure in accordance with the policy. It is not designed to question financial or business decisions taken by the University unless these involve malpractice, nor may it be used to reconsider any personal matters which have already been addressed under disciplinary, capability, or complaints procedures, or student conduct procedures. Individuals who make disclosures where there is no public interest will not be protected under this policy and may not be protected under the Act and the disclosure will not be investigated through this policy. 4. Roles and responsibilities 4.1 Employees are responsible for:  raising any concerns in the first instance with their line manager or formally with the University Secretary  ensuring the concern raised is not of a personal nature and therefore a grievance 4.2 Line managers are responsible for:  hearing
answer:
Under the University of Essex Whistleblowing Policy, concerns or complaints can be made if they involve serious wrongdoing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure. Specifically:

- The concern must be in the public interest.
- It should tend to show malpractice.
- Disclosure is made in accordance with these procedures.

The policy protects individuals who make such disclosures from detriment provided certain conditions are met, as detailed in sections 9 and 13 of the document. Personal matters or financial/business decisions not involving malpractice are outside the scope of this policy. Sources: [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf]

Yes the issue is of a personal nature No the issue is not of a personal nature  Use Grievance (.pdf) or Complaints Procedures (.pdf)  Is the disclosure of serious wrong doing that has an impact beyond the individual making the disclosure?  Make disclosure to the University Secretary (or alternative set out in the Policy) in writing with background setting out why this is in the public
answer:
After a concern is disclosed under this policy, the following steps typically occur:

1. The designated person will acknowledge the disclosure in writing within 10 working days.
2. Depending on the nature of the disclosure, they may discuss next steps with People and Culture or another senior independent member of staff.
3. Next steps might include:
   - An investigation under the University’s Conducting Investigations Guidance
   - An ‘ad hoc’ investigation
   - A referral to the police
   - No further action if the concern is judged to be without merit

Sources: [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/policy-whistleblowing.pdf]

answer:
The stages involved in raising a complaint under this procedure are:

1. **Early Resolution** - Seeks to resolve concerns swiftly at the point where they are raised, or as close to that point as possible.
2. **Formal Complaint** - Investigated by an independent Complaint Investigator appointed by the Student Progress Team if early resolution is not satisfactory or not possible.
3. **Internal Review** - Provides an opportunity for a formal review of the process from the formal complaint stage to ensure appropriate procedures were followed and that the outcome was reasonable.
4. **External Review** - Can be requested by the complainant within twelve months of receiving the Completion of Procedures letter, through the Office of the Independent Adjudicator (OIA).

answer:
The University ensures upholding these values through several steps:

1. Ensuring access to premises for activities aligned with the University's charitable objects, controlled by the University or its members, or authorized through the Students' Union.
2. Protecting academic staff’s freedom within the law to question and test received wisdom, and to express new ideas and controversial opinions without fear of losing their jobs or privileges.
3. Not denying access to University premises for invited external speakers unless their expression is unlawful and anticipated to cause significant disruption.

answer:
The University ensures promotion of core values through several means as outlined in the Code of Practice:

1. **Promoting Academic Freedom and Freedom of Speech**: The University recognizes these freedoms as fundamental commitments, essential for an inclusive community. It encourages lawful expression of diverse ideas and opinions to enable all members to feel safe expressing their views.

2. **Decision-Making Framework and Protocols**: There are established frameworks and protocols to guide decision-making processes related to academic freedom and freedom of speech.

3. **Speaker Protocol**: The University has a protocol for speakers, including rights and responsibilities, risk assessments, and approval processes for external speakers.

4. **Supporting Academic Staff**: Academic staff have the freedom within the law to question received wisdom and put forward new ideas without fear of losing their jobs or privileges.

5. **Encouraging Debate and Discussion**: The University supports environments where intense inquiry and informed argument can flourish, fostering a culture that values rationality in knowledge pursuit.

6. **Handling Counter-Speech and Protest**: While the right to protest is acknowledged, it does not infringe upon others' rights to freedom of speech within the law.

These measures collectively aim to uphold academic integrity and inclusivity at the University of Essex. Sources: [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/cop-freedom-of-speech.pdf], [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/speaker-code-of-practice.pdf]

label: code-practice-professional-doctorates.pdf[primary]
question: What is the frequency of review for departmental arrangements related to Professional Doctorates, and when are changes necessary?
context:
[source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/code-practice-professional-doctorates.pdf] [title: code-practice-professional-doctorates.pdf] [doc_type: policy] [department: n/a] [academic_year: 2025-26]
Centre or Partner Institution with professional doctorates students should: 1.1 Professional Doctorate Director Designate a Director for each Professional Doctorate, a group of professional doctorates, or equivalent, who is an experienced doctoral student supervisor. The Director will be responsible to the Head of Department (HoD) for the departmental arrangements for the relevant PD students and for liaising with and reporting as necessary to the Dean. 1.2 Departmental arrangements Ensure that on acceptance of an offer of admission (that is normally well before registration) all new PD students are supplied with the details of the departmental arrangements for PD students. The departmental documentation and the arrangements it describes should be appropriate for the degree programme concerned and should be approved by the Dean. Departmental documentation will be reviewed every three years or sooner if the department wishes to make any major changes to the arrangements or if any changes to University policies, procedures or regulations requires it. supervisors (see section 4 below) Ensure that adequate arrangements are made for research supervision when any of the research supervisors either leaves the Trust or is on an extended period of leave (for example a form of parental leave or sabbatical) and is not carrying on with supervision during this period. Any changes in supervision must be approved by the Course Lead. Any permanent changes, as well as arrangements for replacement supervision where a member of staff is on leave, must also be reported to the Postgraduate Research Education Team as soon as possible. 1.11 Research supervision and the frequency of supervisory meetings Agree suitable norms regarding the frequency of formal supervisory meetings between PD students and research supervisors and ensure they are followed by supervisors. These will probably vary according to the discipline and the stage that the student has reached. However, students and supervisors are required to communicate, whether face-to-face or by electronic means, to engage in discussion/review of the student's work and progress at least once a month (bi-monthly for part-time students).

answer: Students should sign that they have read and understood the documentation, as per the policies from multiple academic years (2018-19, 2020-21, 2025-26). [source_url: https://www.essex.ac.uk/-/media/documents/about/governance/postgraduate-research-integrity-and-research-misconduct-policy.pdf] [title: postgraduate-research-integrity-and-research-misconduct-policy.pdf] [doc_type: policy] [department: n/a] [academic_year: 2025-26]
Research Practice must be drawn to the attention of all postgraduate researchers at an early stage, and forms part of an essential briefing from the supervisor(s) and general induction. Each postgraduate research (PGR) student will be required to sign-off to say they have read and understood the Code of Good Research Practice as part of their first Supervisory Panel report. 3. The University expects all students: ◼ to carry out their research with rigour, respect, and integrity ◼ to behave with honesty and integrity in relation to coursework, examinations, and other assessed work ◼ to be familiar and act in accordance with the conventions of academic writing including appropriate referencing of sources and acknowledgement of assistance ◼ to show understanding of ethical considerations and be compliant with the relevant University Procedures 4. Failure by a PGR student to comply with the provisions of the Code of Good Research Practice may be grounds for action under this Procedure and may also be subject to action under the University’s disciplinary procedures. 21 Other relevant external policies 22 Key contacts 23 Postgraduate Research Integrity and Research Misconduct Policy Page 3 of 24 Procedure for the investigation of allegations of misconduct in Postgraduate Research A. Introduction 1. The University of Essex is committed to undertaking high quality research and knowledge exchange in an environment of high standards of research integrity, governance, and good practice. The University’s Code of Good Research Practice sets out the expectations of all who engage in or support our research and knowledge exchange (‘researchers’) in and/or for the University. Those responsible for postgraduate research students conducting research have a particular duty to ensure that this group ‘researchers in training’ receive appropriate training in the ethical and research integrity, such that they are made aware of good practice in the conduct of and dissemination of the results of research. 2. The University’s Code of Good Research Practice must be drawn to the attention of all postgraduate researchers at an early stage, and forms part of an essential briefing from the supervisor(s) and general induction. Each

time. 2.5 Academic Offences Supervisors must encourage their students to read the Academic Offences Policy (.pdf), the Research Integrity and Research Misconduct Procedures (.pdf) and the Code of Good Research Practice (.pdf). Supervisors are encouraged to conduct formative exercises to run student’s work through plagiarism detection software in their first term of study.are encouraged to conduct formative exercises to run student’s work through plagiarism detection software in their first term of study. The University reserves the right to use all legitimate means at its disposal to detect instances of plagiarism. Code of Practice Postgraduate Research Degrees 2025-26 Page 8 of 23 2.6 Accessibility, leave and supervisory changes Be reasonably accessible to students and advise them of any lengthy absences which will change the routine of monthly meetings. Keep each student well informed in advance about any prospective periods of leave and the planned supervisory arrangements during the leave. 2.7 Update knowledge and skills Ensure that their knowledge and skills are as up-to-date as possible. kept on the student’s file confirming the arrangements that have been agreed in the absence of face-to-face contact. 2.3 Research guidance Provide guidance about the nature and standard of research work expected, including advice on attaining that standard, together with advice on academic practice in the discipline, matters of research design, ethical issues, and appropriate health and safety issues. Supervisors should make it clear to each research student that it is possible for the research and writing up to be completed within the standard period and provide guidance on the appropriate volume of research work for the degree in question. They should also assist each research student in developing a clear timetable of work and emphasise the importance of keeping to it. 2.4 Written work, oral presentations and feedback Request written work and oral presentations as appropriate and comment on such work within a reasonable time. 2.5 Academic Offences Supervisors must encourage their students to read the Academic Offences Policy (.pdf), the Research Integrity and Research Misconduct Procedures (.pdf) and the Code of Good Research

Code of Practice Postgraduate Research Degrees 2025-26 Page 6 of 23 1.15 Staff induction and training Ensure that all new members of staff who may become research student supervisors are seen by the Postgraduate Research Director to discuss the University Code of Practice and that all supervisors are supplied with a copy of the Department’s supervisory arrangements every year. The Department should also ensure that less experienced members of staff who have not supervised a PhD through to completion attend the relevant University’s training for research student supervisors. The Department should ensure that opportunities for sharing good practice in supervision are included in the departmental and central arrangements for training and support provided to all academic staff, including any mentoring provision. 1.16 Academic networking Ensure that research students are advised of the importance of making contact with other researchers in the field and of presenting their work to academic audiences. The University of Essex Professional Clinical Appearance Code of Practice aims to provide clarity on how this diversity is recognised alongside the requirements placed on students during their studies at the University of Essex, both when on campus for academic learning, and when on practice placement with a partner organisation. This guidance is not intended to replace national legislation or policy, or local organisation policy. It is designed to provide clarity to students on what may be considered ‘standard’ expectation, and how this may be reasonably adjusted when on campus,

Students should be aware that as part of their study and the preparation for a clinical workplace, students will ultimately need to respect and comply with both University Code of Conduct, and local policies in place at partner organisations. answer:
If a student does not follow the University of Essex Professional Clinical Appearance Code of Practice, particularly during placements, they may be considered to be in breach of the University of Essex Code of Conduct (relating to safety on University premises) and the responsibility to follow relevant regulatory and/or professional body codes of practice. This non-compliance could lead to students being referred for investigation by the Conduct Team, and potentially referred to the Professional Suitability Group as per Fitness to Practise procedures.

answer:
According to the policy, students can apply for academic flexibility if they are dual-career athletes and face conflicts between their sporting commitments and academic study. Specifically:

- They must be made aware of selection or possible selection for national training camps, international competitions, or representing University of Essex teams in professional or BUCS competitions that interfere with their studies.
- Students should complete Section A of the Application for Academic Flexibility Form and submit it along with supporting evidence to the Sports Performance Team within six weeks after the start of the academic year.

Additionally, students can apply for assessment flexibility if they are made aware of selection or possible selection for an International or BUCS competition that clashes with examination or coursework deadlines. They must then complete a similar form and submit it to the Sports Performance Team.

answer:
According to the Student Protection Plan, some risks that could affect the delivery of education at the University of Essex include:

- Financial insolvency (risk of not being financially sustainable)
- Course changes or discontinuation
- Closure of the department offering your course
- Campus closure where your course is taught
- Insufficient IT infrastructure for course delivery
- Lack of teaching staff
- Building or campus issues preventing teaching events
- Loss of right to offer qualifications
- Loss of right to teach international students
- Poor risk management

The Student Protection Plan is a document, approved by the Office for Students, which every university must have in place. It explains how the University manages risk in relation to the education we deliver, and the actions that we would take if a risk to the continuation of your studies arose. This plan should be read alongside the Student Terms and Conditions of the offer you have accepted for your place at our university, which
answer:
The University of Essex has several mitigation strategies in place to address the risk of financial insolvency:

- Operating within a clear financial framework.
- Achieving an annual minimum cash surplus of 5.5%.
- Ensuring earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation are at least twice annual debt servicing costs.
- Maintaining minimum liquid funds equal to at least 60 days of expenditure.
- Requiring all Departments/Schools to generate a cash surplus or have recovery plans to ensure break-even within three years.
- Only funding projects from external borrowing where servicing costs can be met by additional income or savings.

These strategies are monitored regularly by the University Council, Policy and Resources Committee, and Audit and Risk Management Committee. The University has also tested its financial sustainability against a worst-case student numbers scenario to maintain education and research capacity.

answer:
The policy ensures fairness by treating all students who experience health or mental health difficulties fairly, sensitively, and with respect. It aims to provide support and guidance to help them engage and continue their studies. Additionally, the University has a responsibility under the Equality Act (2010) not to discriminate against disabled students, including those with mental health problems, and to make reasonable adjustments to ensure they are not placed at a significant disadvantage.

Policy 1. Applications 1. To make an application under this policy prior to commencing studies at Essex, the standard Admissions application form should be used. Information about how to apply for all applicants can be found on the Undergraduate, Postgraduate Taught and Postgraduate Research applying webpages. 2. Applications for Recognition of Prior Learning (RPL) can take place either before a student joins the University of Essex or during the course of their study. Where the learning took place before the student applied to a University of Essex course, the application for RPL should be made before the student starts their study on the course into which they are seeking to transfer prior learning to wherever possible. Applications for RPL can be made during a course where required but cannot be used to replace a module in which a student has enrolled and been unsuccessful. 3. Applications for RPL prior to commencing studies at Essex can be made as part of a standard application for a University of Essex course. Postgraduate research students should discuss their

of prior learning (RPL) encompasses APL. In addition, RPL recognises prior learning that has occurred in a range of educational and training contexts and/or where learning is achieved outside education or training systems. This prior learning is recognised for academic purposes either through credit or other formal recognition. These terms may be used differently in specific regulated disciplines, such as nursing and other healthcare professions. Recognition of prior learning is referred to by the abbreviation RPL throughout this policy. Staged awards A ‘staged award’ is a discrete award that a student applies for and receives when they progress from one course to another, requiring study at a particular level and achieving a minimum number of credits, for example a Postgraduate Certificate or Postgraduate Diploma. Scope of the policy Recognition of prior learning occurs where previous study or other forms of learning is recognised. This could be for instance: ◼ credit undertaken at another university is taken into consideration to allow a student to join years 2 or 3 of a 3-year honours degree (which may be referred to as a transfer from another university) ◼ prior learning (for example through professional experience) is assigned a credit value to allow the student not to take specific modules that would normally be included on their course. This is particularly important for students on degree apprenticeship courses, as their training plan should ensure that they do not duplicate knowledge, skills and behaviours they already have within their apprenticeship programme ◼ a student uses existing University of Essex award towards another award (‘re-use of credit’) ◼ prior training and/or research

re-use credit in this way, they would need to be considered under the Readmissions Policy. 5. Calculation of degree results 1. Credit awarded by any other institution will not usually be used in calculating the final taught degree result of any student who has made a successful application for RPL. In such cases, calculated averages or other methods to determine the degree result will be based entirely on University of Essex credit. This does not apply to postgraduate research provision where the work recognised via RPL would form part of the final output. Where exemption is sought from terms of study of a research degree, consideration should be given to: a. confirmation of PhD status b. milestones required c. training needs d. supervisory capacity of the department. 5. For exemption from terms of study of a research degree it should also be considered that ethical approval through University of Essex processes may need to be sought and that this should be considered when agreeing the proposed period of study following RPL. 6. The consideration of all the above factors should be documented with a decision as whether the application for Recognition of Prior Learning can be approved and, if so, a specification of
answer:
The recognition of prior learning (RPL) encompasses various types of learning that can occur in educational and training contexts as well as outside formal education or training systems. This includes:

- Prior study undertaken at another university.
- Professional experience that can be assigned a credit value to allow students not to take specific modules they have already covered through their work experience.
- Existing University of Essex awards used towards another award (re-use of credit).
- Training and/or research conducted outside the University.

They also oversee and regularly review this policy on behalf of the University Steering Group, ensuring its effective implementation and dissemination to staff and students.

answer:
The term 'Capped Mark' refers to a maximum mark that can be achieved for reassessment, which is 40 for modules at Levels 6 and below. For modules at Level 7 and above, the capped mark is 50.

Degree Mark To achieve an Honours Degree, you must attempt 360 credits and pass at least 330 credits across your degree with at least 90 credits at Level 6. All core modules must be passed. Your marks from Year Two and the Final Year are used to calculate an overall Degree Mark. Each is given a percentage weighting (see under ‘Weighting’). For a Three-Year Degree and for a Four-Year Degree where Year Three is a Placement Year, your Year Two mark counts as 40% and your Final Year Mark counts as 60%. You can only fail 30 non-core credits across your degree. For example, if you fail 30 non-core credits in Year One, you cannot fail any more credits in your degree. Carrying forward failed credits to following years is called condonement. Where credits are condoned, the Module Mark will still be used to calculate the Year Mark. To progress from Year One to Year Two in an Integrated Masters programme at the University of Essex, students must:

- Achieve 120 credits at Level 4.
- Pass at least 60 credits at the first attempt.
- Achieve a Year Mark of 50 at the first attempt.
- Pass core modules and meet any additional programme or module specific requirements.

credit lifetime for any module is six years. Some modules have variations to the generic rules which must also be achieved to be awarded the credit. These variations could for example, take the form of a higher pass mark or have an additional requirement to pass individual parts of assessment such as an exam. In such cases, the additional requirement(s) must be met for credit to be awarded. Students usually demonstrate that they have achieved the learning outcomes of a module programme, or qualification by completing one or more pieces of assessed work. To be awarded the credits, a student must reach the minimum standard, known as the pass mark. The pass mark is normally 50 at Level 7, but any variations will be set out in the Rules of Assessment for the programme. The pass mark for Level 5 and 6 modules on the Graduate Diploma and Certificate is 40, as these awards are not at masters’ Level. Any variations will need to be approved and will be published in the Rules of Assessment
answer:
Based on the provided context, for both the Graduate Diploma and Certificate programs in the academic year 2025-26 (the most recent relevant information), students must obtain a minimum of 45 credits at Level 6 to be awarded a certificate. However, the rules allow for some flexibility:

For the Graduate Certificate:
- A student can attempt 60 credits, with at least 45 needing to be passed.
- Up to 20 non-core taught module credits (with a mark of 35 or more) may be condoned if the overall weighted average is 40 or above.

This means that while there must be a minimum of 45 credits at Level 6, some lower-level credits can be included in the total to meet the requirement.

The rules generally require passing more credits to proceed to subsequent years, but they do allow for condonement of up to 30 non-core credits if certain conditions are met.

University of Essex Rules of Assessment for Full-Time Taught Masters Degrees 2025-2026 School of Sport, Rehabilitation and Exercise Sciences MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) (24 months – full-time) 1. Preamble a) These rules apply to the full-time (24 months) Masters programme named above. The rules should be read in conjunction with the relevant Programme Structure, Programme Specification and online Module Directory entries. The Module Directory will give detail of assessment arrangements. The Programme Structure will identify which modules are core for the programme. b) A taught module is any assessed component excluding the dissertation or equivalent, which for this programme is SE760: Research Project. A core module must be taken as part of the programme structure and passed, with no condonement of marks below a pass allowed. c) All Masters degrees can be awarded with Distinction or Merit d) Successful completion of the MSc also leads to eligibility to apply for registration with the Health and Care Professions Council. answer:
For the MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration) program:

- **Pass**: Obtain 150 credits at Masters Level (Level 7), pass all assessment components except SE760: Research Project, and achieve an overall weighted average mark of 60 or more.
- **Merit**: In addition to passing the requirements for a Postgraduate Diploma, have an overall weighted average mark of 60 or more.
- **Distinction**: In addition to passing the requirements for a Postgraduate Diploma, have an overall weighted average mark of 70 or more.

answer:
If a student is unable to complete their studies or does not achieve the required credits to be awarded a Masters, they may be considered for an exit award of either a Postgraduate Diploma or a Postgraduate Certificate. Specifically for the MSc Physiotherapy (Pre-registration), students who do not meet the requirements can still potentially obtain a Postgraduate Diploma if they have 150 credits at Master's Level and pass all assessment components except SE760, as detailed in section 4.2.1 of the document.

credit lifetime for any module is six years. Some modules have variations to the generic rules which must also be achieved to be awarded the credit. These variations could for example, take the form of a higher pass mark or have an additional requirement to pass individual parts of assessment such as an exam. Students usually demonstrate that they have achieved the learning outcomes of a module programme, or qualification by completing one or more pieces of assessed work. To be awarded the credits, a student must reach the minimum standard, known as the pass mark. The pass mark is normally 50 at Level 7, but any variations will be set out in the Rules of Assessment for the programme. The pass mark for Level 5 and 6 modules on the Graduate Diploma and Certificate is 40, as these awards are not at masters’ Level. Marks are rounded to the nearest whole number for the purposes of calculating the overall weighted average. Specifically, a mark of 49.5 will count as 50, while 49.4 will be counted as 49.

answer:
According to the provided documents, the general principles for reassessment include:

- You need to take reassessment if you have failed any module(s) after completing all your assessments but have a Year Mark of 20 or above.
- The Board of Examiners can permit up to two further attempts at any module, with marks capped at 40 unless extenuating circumstances are substantiated.
- You will be offered all the reassessment opportunities available.
- If you attempt reassessment and your mark is lower than a previous attempt, the higher mark will be used to determine progression.

Reassessment Opportunities at the End of the Year 3.10 If you do not pass the Year, but have a Year Mark of 20 or above, you may be offered reassessment. 3.11 If you fail up to 60 credits, you will be offered compulsory reassessment before the start of Year Three at Chula. Your Module Mark(s) will be capped at 40. 3.12 If you fail more than 60 credits you will be offered one or more of the following: (a) A full-time repeat year of Year Two (all modules) for uncapped Module Marks; (b) A part-time repeat year of failed Year Two modules for up to 105 credits for uncapped Module Marks; (c) Reassessment in the following Year without attending the University for capped Module Marks. answer:
According to the documents, if a student does not pass Year One but has a Year Mark of 20 or above, they may be offered compulsory reassessment before the start of Year Two. If the student fails up to 60 credits, they will be offered this reassessment. For failing more than 60 credits, the student can be offered one or more of the following options: a full-time repeat year for all modules, a part-time repeat year for failed modules up to 105 credits, or reassessment in the following Year with capped Module Marks.
